coredata.dc:title: Recent advancement in fluorescent materials for optical sensing of pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Gyanjyoti, Aswini
Guleria, Praveen
Awasthi, Abhishek
Singh, Kulvinder
Kumar, Vineet
coredata.dc:description: Pesticides are widely used in agricultural and non-agricultural sectors posing, risks to human health as well as the ecological system. Therefore, inexpensive and convenient detection methods are required to detect pesticides in biological samples. The demand for fluorescent-based nano-sensor has proved to be a rapid along with reliable method for quantification of pesticides and gaining attention in various fields including chemo-sensors, biosensors, bioimaging, and so on. It has been considered a promising and effective tool in monitoring harmful and small analytes and possessing unique selectivity, high sensitivity, low cost and ecofriendly. Several optical sensing methodologies have been employed for the detection, such as nanoparticles, quantum dots, carbon dots, metal nanoparticles. In addition, nanoparticles based aptasensors, antibodies, aggregation induced emission, metal organic frameworks, ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe, photoluminescence metal-organic frameworks, fluorescent molecular imprinted polymers, paper-based analytical device, electrochemiluminescence sensors, and fluorescent probe based on Cucurbit[n]urils have emerged as the new class of fluorescent materials with great potential in the field of optical sensing. This review gives a brief description of types of pesticides, strategies for detecting the pesticides, properties, advantages and disadvantages, and discussed some references to enhance the ability and usability of the fluorescent based nanosensors.

head.ce:title: Recent advancement in fluorescent materials for optical sensing of pesticides

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pesticides are widely used in agricultural and non-agricultural sectors posing, risks to human health as well as the ecological system. Therefore, inexpensive and convenient detection methods are required to detect pesticides in biological samples. The demand for fluorescent-based nano-sensor has proved to be a rapid along with reliable method for quantification of pesticides and gaining attention in various fields including chemo-sensors, biosensors, bioimaging, and so on. It has been considered a promising and effective tool in monitoring harmful and small analytes and possessing unique selectivity, high sensitivity, low cost and ecofriendly. Several optical sensing methodologies have been employed for the detection, such as nanoparticles, quantum dots, carbon dots, metal nanoparticles. In addition, nanoparticles based aptasensors, antibodies, aggregation induced emission, metal organic frameworks, ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe, photoluminescence metal-organic frameworks, fluorescent molecular imprinted polymers, paper-based analytical device, electrochemiluminescence sensors, and fluorescent probe based on Cucurbit[n]urils have emerged as the new class of fluorescent materials with great potential in the field of optical sensing. This review gives a brief description of types of pesticides, strategies for detecting the pesticides, properties, advantages and disadvantages, and discussed some references to enhance the ability and usability of the fluorescent based nanosensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last sixty years, pesticides are being insistently used over worldwide, indeed to inhibit, diminish and terminate the pest. Pesticides are more frequently used in agricultural sectors as well as in non-agricultural regions like garden, house, etc., leads to contaminating soil, underground water and food that can cause damage to the human health as well as ecological system [1]. Worldwide it is evaluated that the utilization of pesticides is approximately 2 million tons that includes herbicides (47.5%), insecticides (29.5%), fungicides (17.5%), and other pesticides (5.5%) [2]. According to Research and Markets, the foremost producers and consumers of pesticides is India with the worth of 197 billion INR. It is further estimation that this market will cross 316 billions in the coming four years, with the growth rate of 8.1% (2019–2024) [3]. Globally, the market size of pesticides estimated to grow to reach at a CARG of 11.5% to nearly $130.7 billion by 2023. Pesticides can be categorized into herbicides (treatment of weeds), insecticides (treatment of insects), rodenticides (treatment of rodents), fungicides (treatment of fungus), molluscicides (treatment of molluscs), nematocides (treatment of nematodes), larvicides (treatment of larvae), and acaricides (treatment of ticks and mites) as depicted in Fig. 1 [4].

Chemically pesticides are classified as organophosphorus (OPs), carbamate, organochlorine (OC), pyrethroid, sulfonylurea, and glyphosate (GLY) that possesses high toxic elements and poisonous compounds as depicted in Fig. 2.

These pesticides lead to several health issues that include headache, cancer, anorexia, stimulate the vagal nerve ending, and even death [5–8]. Both the government and privates sectors took an initiative to spread awareness about the proper utilization of pesticides, including the best quality and quantity to be used and validity or legitimacy of usage and outcomes. Therefore, reliable and inexpensive qualitative as well as quantitative methodologies are required to monitor the concentration level of these pesticides in various systems.

Many conventional analytical methods were exerted over the past decades for proper monitoring of the concentration of pesticides that includes high-performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, electrochemical analysis [9], liquid-solid extraction [10], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, surface plasmon resonance, X-ray diffraction, capillary electrophoresis, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [11]. Although these methods have some merits such as high sensitivity, selectivity, accuracy, and reliability [12], at the same time these methods includes various demerits including time-consuming, tedious pre-treatment process, expensive, complicated operation, expensive chemicals, and required trained personnel [13,14]. Considering these drawbacks, a facile, rapid, sensitive, selective, cost effective, expedient, and simple method is still an urgent exigency for the qualitative and quantitative assessment of pesticides.

In the recent years, nanoparticles (NPs) based sensors have proven to a promising and suitable candidate for the monitoring of pesticides due to their unmatched properties especially time to assess, reliability, cost effectiveness, reproducibility, high-throughput and above all convenient in comparison to other methodologies [9,15–20]. Many detection methods based on nanomaterials were implemented, for instance, electrochemical analysis, Raman, colorimetric detection, and photoluminescence (PL) techniques [21–26]. Among these NPs based sensing techniques, PL-based nano-sensor (such as carbon dots and graphene quantum dots) has drawn more attention and it is one of the reliable, efficient, sensitive, cost effective analytical technique [10,16,27–34]. In addition to this PL sensing platform is a promising sensing method that reduces the background signals and amplify sensitivity by blend of excitation and emission [14,35]. However, the majority of reported PL sensors sought to find metal cations (mostly for heavy metal ions) and it has considered being one of the effective tools in monitoring harmful and small analytes and possess obvious advantages such as high quantum yields, high photostability with narrow emission, miniaturized instruments, simple and on-site operation, real-time imaging, and low cost [20,30,36]. But most of the fluorescent techniques suffer from some inherent limitations such as photo bleaching, poor water solubility, toxic and short lifetimes, which have intrinsically confined their further applications [26,37]. However still, fluorescent-based nano-sensors are in great demand, utilized for the detection of chemically composed pesticide residues, harmful toxins, and contaminants, mostly in the field of the food industry and other agricultural sectors [38–42]. In this context, NPs have been widely explored as optical sensors for the detection of pesticides. The major issues while using the pesticides is the poor selectivity of the NPs towards a specific pesticide that leads to generate the false signals. To further improve the selectivity various chemical and biochemicals have been decorated on the surface of NPs that either creates an impression or cavity of the specific pesticide that improves the selectivity.

Therefore, in the present review, various fluorescent-based nano-sensors have been explored for the monitoring the pesticides. The demand for fluorescent nano sensors that can be used for the rapid detection of harmful toxins and components in drinking water, underground water fruits, and vegetables will also be discussed. The main focus of this review is to deliver a description of a few classes of pesticides and its harmful effects on humans, animals, and the environment, and a thorough analysis is provided on the notable advancements in optical sensors for detecting pesticides over with a focus on fluorescent materials, features using PL, and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) by integrating knowledge of the sensing mechanisms and design principles with numerous recognition components, such as aptamers, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), and NPs. Moreover, some survey have been provided to inspire new and advanced research methods for detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different classes of pesticides based on the chemical composition

ce:section.ce:para: OPs are one of the sub-class of agrochemical pesticides that are broadly used in food crops as insecticides [43]. OPs are generally esters and amides, consisting of thiophosphoryl functional (P = S) and (P = O) groups [44]. Humans and animals are exposed to OPs through consumption, by direct contact with contaminants, and by inhaling due to which unalterable inhibition of activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) that accumulates in central nervous system, synaptic cleft at muscarinic and nicotinic, which leads to neural disorders like Parkinson’s disease, respiratory and muscle failure, headache, nausea, paralysis and even death [5,45,46]. It not only affects living beings but also have hazardous effect on ecosystem [47]. Diazinon, Parathion, Malathion, Nitroaromatic compounds, and GLY are the most commonly used OPs.

Diazinon chemically known as O, O-diethyl-O-(2-isopropyl-4-methyl-6 pyrimidinyl) phosphorothioate generally used in agricultural and non-agricultural sectors against insecticides [48,49]. Still it carries various harmful effects to health and the environment [50,51]. Traces of toxic diazinon on heat, lymph nodes, thymus, hepatocytes, blood cells, and spleen can be found in humans and animals that causes cancer like leukemia [5].

Methyl parathion or Parathion-methyl (MP) and ethyl parathion also known as thiophos, are mostly used pesticides and due to their bioaccumulation in food and water, gives concerns about food safety and quality of drinking water. Exposure or intake of this toxic element leads to hematological diseases where hemoglobin count is low, shows RBC contraction, disablement of heme biosynthesis, and also includes attention deficit and hyperactivity disorder [52–54].

Malathion chemically known as Diethyl 2- dimethoxy phosphinothiosulfanyl butane dioate with the molecular formula C10H19O6PS2 used to prevent pests on crops such as tea, tobacco, vegetables, rice, and mulberry. Various reports are present in literature that reveals malathion is teratogenic, carcinogenic, mutagenic, and also gives rise to endocrine disruption and neurotoxicity [55,56].

Nitroaromatic compounds p-nitrophenol, dinitrotoluene, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, 2,4- and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene are extensively used for the production of dyes, manufacture of drugs, in leather, rubber additives, pesticides, and explosives for military purposes [57,58]. These toxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic compounds have been found in wastewater discharged from industry, coking, refining, metallurgy, petrochemical, chemical organic synthesis, and contaminating water resources. Exposure to these compounds induces various diseases causing systemic poisoning, skin and eye irritation, anemia, liver and kidney damage [59,60].

GLY is a postemergence, broad-spectrum OPs, a non-selective and non-systematic herbicide that is widely used in agriculture, forestry, urban and residential purposes to inhibit broadleaf weeds and for vegetation control [61–63]. Most glyphosate-based formulations , such as roundup was introduced to the market by monsanto company for agriculture use. Its chemical composition is phosphonate and GLY herbicides and the activity of 5-enolpyruvylshikimate-3-phosphate synthase, enzyme of the shikimate route which is essential for the production of three aromatic amino acids i.e. tryptophan, tyrosine, and phenylalanine in plants [63–65]. To eliminate the halt, Monsanto company has also generated genetically modified GLY-resistant crops also called Roundup Ready crops. In comparison to other herbicides, GLY is relatively low toxicity to mammals, due to which it has become the most frequently used herbicide all around the world. But according to some records, the indiscriminate application of glyphosate-based formulations may lead to high-level residues in agricultural products, detected in air during spraying, expected to be absorbed by the soil and contaminating surface and groundwater, which create a potential threat and is reported carcinogenic to human health. Recent studies suggest that it is a potential endocrine disruptor, cause neurological disorder and can induce cell death through apoptosis and autophagy [66–71].

Carbamate is an ester derived from carbamic acid, due to its high effectiveness and low persistence it is used to exterminate a wide spectrum of insects and pests [58,72]. Carbamates like carbofuran, thiram, ethyl carbamate, and carbaryl are mostly utilized in the agricultural domain to elevate the production of crop. Carbofuran, thiram (Fungicide) are used to protect the food crops like fruits and vegetables such as peaches, apples, grapes, pears, tomatoes, strawberries, etc. ethyl carbamate belongs to a 2 A carcinogen group, can be found in fermented foods and alcoholic beverages. Carbaryl (1-napthyl methylcarbamate), an insecticide that acts as a growth regulator and can be found in soil [73–75]. These residues are genotoxic, carcinogenic, and mutagenic which leads to the disruption of the endocrine system, immunological disorder, infertility, nerve and bone marrow disorder, anorexia, and the effect of carbaryl leads to damage on DNA and the creation of chromosome aberration [7,76,77].

Organochlorine is a class of chlorine containing lethal compounds used as pesticides and fumigants. Organochlorine are considered to be persistent organic pollutants because of their ability to persist in the ecosystems for an extended duration and don’t disappear once they are used for crop production [78]. The widely use of pesticides has caused many adverse effects on humans for instance cancer and disruption of the central nervous system. Moreover, the residues of organochlorine s are highly effective, constituting a major environmental concern. Most of the country has banned the production of organochlorines in agricultural sectors due to possessing high chemical stability and bioaccumulation inside the living body [79–82].

Pyrethroids pesticides, as highly efficient and commercial insecticides, are broadly used in agriculture and extensively employed in residential settings such as in gardens, animal houses, and greenhouses. It possesses low toxicity, high efficiency, rapid action, high stability, and wide resistance to the pest [57,83,84]. Pyrethroids are usually present in several consumer products, that includes lice treatments, pet shampoos, mosquito repellents, garden insecticides. In addition it is also applied to cloths as well as to manage pests such as fruits, vegetables, soyabeans, corns, cotton etc. [83,85,86]. The recent studies reveal that these pesticides may entered into the human body by various food chains and their residues may cause congenic malformation, lymphadenopathy, reproductive damage, nerve damage, cellular damage, genetic damage, and also may cause neural harm to animals as well as children and infants, Overexposure to pyrethroid may cause hyperactivity disorder and brain tumors in children [87–90].

Sulfonylureas herbicides, are a comparatively new group of ecologically compatible pesticides which are relatively used to selectively control not only pre-emergence but also for the post-emergence weed in farmland. It inhibits the multiplication and cell growth of plant cells by blocking the biogenesis of branched-chain amino acids. Sulfonylureas herbicides have been developed, with orthosulfamuron, mesosulfuron-methyl, halosulfuron-methyls, and ethoxysulfuron. these pesticides are all used to control broadleaved weeds in different cereals as well as vegetable crops such as wheat, rice, maize, barley, potato, tomato, and so on [63,91,92]. Sulfonylureas herbicides have gained a lot of attention widely as a class of extraordinary herbicide due to their low dosage requirement, good crop selectivity, and low mammalian toxicity. Although Sulfonylureas herbicides are beneficial for agriculture but recent studies reveal that there is ecological risks for microorganisms, aquatic systems, crops, non-targeted organisms, polluting soil, underground water due to their increasing use on agricultural use and long term application [55,93,94]. As the high efficiency of these herbicides, the low concentration of them is detected in soil and water due to their high mobility. Humans get indirectly exposed to the herbicides through residues in certain food and drinking water causing adverse effects to humans such as the formation of urinary calculus. According to some recent reports Sulfonylureas herbicides possess high phytotoxicity and is a major source of aquatic environment contamination by the laboratory exposure [95–97].

Aptamers, an artificial antibody, are single-stranded oligonucleotides with specific RNA or DNA sequence peptides that are designated from the random-sequence nucleic acid libraries, but generally synthesized form a strategy known to be systematic evolution of ligands by exponential (SELEX). These aptamers can be employed to use as biosensors for the detection of different targets such as drugs, proteins, viruses or even whole cell, metal ions, pesticides and toxicants with high affinity and specificity [5,49,64,98]. In comparison to natural antibodies, aptamers are inexpensive, easy to modify, highly stable, can be easily synthesized and functionalized, fold reversible, fold into the specific structure, and super specific binding with target molecules, it can be combined with different optical active NPs by various techniques such as optical, electrical, photochemical, and biological, applied in bio sensing technology to develop advanced aptasensor for target detection [55,99–102]. In addition, to control the affinity of the aptamer with the target molecules, the functionalization process is tune for the decoration of aptamers with the fluorophores [103]. Most importantly, the key factor for enhancing the selectivity by the aptamer based sensors to a specific target is the key-lock mechanism arises mainly due to the several types of interactions i.e. H bonding, electrostatic interaction, Vander waal forces [104]. It has been reported in literature that aptamers are proven to the advantageous in terms of affinity, stability as regenrability as compare to the antibodies [105]. These results indicates that the aptamers has been considered as promising candidate for the diagnosing the pollutant specially pesticides [106].

In 2011, first SELEX strategy was applied from single stranded DNA to develop the aptamer for pesticide detection i.e. acetamiprid [107]. Talari et al., prepared a conjugate of aptamer with reduced graphene quantum dots (QDs) as an probe along with multi-walled carbon nanotubes solution for the efficient monitoring of diazinon [5]. The developed aptasensor for diazinon detection have examined for real sample such as agricultural runoff, river and tap water. In addition, human urine was collected and spiked with five different concentrations of analyte. The interaction of aptamer with analyte leads to the enhancement of fluorescent signal and the sensing parameters comes out to be 0.4 nM (limit of detection) with 4.0 nM as quantification limit. The linear range was 4–31 nM of diazinon along with 97% reproducible accuracy. The obtained aptasensor was verified and the response was assessed in the presence of other interfering pesticides (fenthion, dichlorvos, bentazon, pirimicard, malathion, and deltamethrin), and later the aptasensor shows no interference of other pesticides in the response of diazinon. Based on the obtained result, it suggests the developed aptasensor has the ability to detect multi-pesticides with high sensitivity, good reproducibility, portable non-toxic biosensor, and can detect onsite with high stability, specificity, and instability ( Fig. 3).

Similarly, Wang et al., 2012 have reported the DNA aptamer that shows great selectivity towards binding with four different pesticides namely phorate, omethoate, profenofos and isocarbophos [15]. In another report S-18 aptamer have been synthesized for the qualitative and quantitative assessment of acetamiprid pesticide [72,108]. Yang et al., λ-cyhalothrin sensitive aptamer (LCT-1) that can precisely bind with λ-cyhalothrin (binding constant of 50.64 ± 4.33 nmol L−1). Fig. 4 represents the secondary structure of LCT-1 along with the molecular docking of λ-cyhalothrin with the fabricated aptamer [109].

The mechanism of these aptamer based optical sensors is the physical adsorption of aptamers onto the surface fluorophores, aptamers binds to the specific target analyte that leads to the quenching in the photoluminescence intensity [110]. Based on this mechanism Lin et al., developed a turn On-Off sensors to the detection of acetamiprid using aptamer modified ZnS:Mn QDs [111]. In this study the PL signal of aptamer modified QDs are turned OFF in presence of MWCNT by the FRET mechanism (due to Π- Π interaction) but as soon as acetamiprid is introduced in the system, the signals turn ON due to its higher affinity with aptamer as compare to the affinity of MWCNT with aptamer. The present study reports the detection limit of 0.7 nmol L-1. Similarly, aptamer modified Au NPs have also been explored for the detection of OPs as well as acetamiprid [112,113]. Rong et al., constructed graphene oxide (GO) with aptamer modified upconversion nanoparticle (Apt-UCNPs) for the detection diazinon, where they have incubated a series of concentrations and the upconversion fluorescence spectra were scanned [48]. The Optimal concentration of GO has obtained when the fluorescence quenching efficiency was maximum, and the sensor has been created using the optimum condition which was then exposed with the different concentrations of diazinon and monitored using fluorescence [48]. Further the monitoring of the pesticide has been examined by assessing the spiked samples, tea, apples, and tap water were taken for the preparation and investigation. After the extraction, filtration, making it supersaturated, salting-out and centrifugation, the unwanted impurities were removed, and lastly, all the solution was diluted to different concentrations for the detection of diazinon. For the optimization of detection conditions, some parameters have been optimized that may affect the detection results. The fluorescence intensity of Apt-UCNPs was measured at 547 nm quenching high efficiency with decreased fluorescence intensity and the calculated LOD of diazinon was come out to be 0.023 ng/mL with linear range from 0.1 to 500 ng/mL. In this study, the RSD to determine the constancy, dependability, and repeatability of the UCNPs-aptamer- GO platform, where the RSD of intra-assay at 547 nm was 5% and the inter-assay was 7,8% which indicates the high stability and high detection sensitivity. The RSD of intra-assay was in a range of 3.43–4.58% and the inter-assay was in a range of 1.18%− 8.02% which indicates the excellent applicability of the nano-sensor. Saleh et al. have fabricated UCNPs based novel optical chemosensor for monitoring metribuzin pesticide with LOD of 68 nM (linear response range is from 0.23 to 1.5 μM and response time 7 min) [114] ( Fig. 5).

The strategies discussed in above section i.e. aptamers based optical sensors, majorly governed by the high selectivity as well specificity towards the target analyte arises due to its strong binding efficiency towards the specific target molecule. Although this technique shows a greater potential in the field of detecting pesticides still functioned nucleic acid or aptamer based “Turn-On” photoluminescent sensors carries specific challenges, particularly complicated synthetic route or high cost probe synthesis of aptamers and recognizing small molecular targets is a major drawback [38,115–117].

Antibodies are the biochemical specially known for its ability to recognize the specific entity brings new gleam in the field of sensing with highly specificity [118–120]. The reason for its high specificity mainly due to its high binding affinity or high association constant [121]. With the advancement in the field of optical luminescence properties of NPs new pathways have been developed for the advancement of antibodies based PL sensors. In this context, QDs was successfully labelled with secondary antibodies to monitor the concentration of sulfamethazine present in chicken muscle [122]. Further, CdTe QDs were conjugated with antibodies were fabricated for the sensing application of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid [123]. Several other pesticides such as parathions, glyphosate, chlorpyrifos have been successfully quantified with the labeling of nanomaterials with antibodies [124–126]. The further improve the applicability of antibodies based PL sensors and for onsite monitoring immune-chromatographic strips have developed by decorating with antibodies for the monitoring of OPs metabolites [127]. Similarly Zhou et al., fabricated phosmet sensor onto the poly (dimethyl siloxane) and the present sensor is implemented on the detection of phosmet on the apple surface [128].

Although optical active nanomaterials hybrid with antibodies finds a new way of sensing methodologies by higher selectivity and specificity with better performance still there are several challenges in the implementation of these antibodies based PL sensors specially cost, tedious fabrication route of antibodies, biocompatibilities of the optical probe etc. [106].

AIEgens emerged as a promising PL strain for various biological applications due its sensing mechanism [129–133]. Many PL sensors woks on the mechanism of excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo induced electron transfer (PET) chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) [134–143]. Various optical probes shows maximum intensity in solution phase and the PL diminishes when the probe aggregates or present in solid state and is known as aggregate induced quenching arises due to Π- Π stacking or non-radiative decay [144–146]. To overcome this issue, AIE emerged as a new class of probe that shows minimum or negligible emission in solution state but shows prominent signals in aggregated state or solid state. This exception response arises due to the restriction in intramolecular rotation as well as vibrations as presently firstly in 2001 [147,148]. Chen et al., have developed the paper based analytical methodologies for the detection of OPs using luminogen@MnO2 core-shell nanomaterials with detection limit of 1.60 g/mL. The mechanism of present sensor is the prevention of fabrication of thiocholine that quenches the reduction of MnO2 [148].

Wu et al., has worked on the visual detection of OPs pesticides using AIEgens-SiO₂-MnO₂ sandwich nanocomposites fabricated with 1,2-bis[4-(3-sulfonatetopropoxyl) phenyl]− 1,2-diphenyl ether. Tap water and river water, taken as real samples. And they have taken paraoxon as a representative to study the performance of the method, including the LOD, recovery, and selectivity [133]. The paraoxon recovery in spiked tap and river water was 97.9–104.4% and the RSDs were 2.1–8.8%, the LOD was found out to be 1 µg/L and the fabrication snore for paraoxon determination has a food linear relationship in the range of 1–100 µg/L, the result reveals that the proposed method is applicable to detect the OPs in environmental water samples. Wang et al., have developed a AIE probe with aid of peptide functionalized tetraphenylethylene for the detection of OPs [149]. OPs covalently interacts with the serine sites of the proteins that leads to the enhancement in aggregation of the proteins thus enhancing the PL emission of the aggregated probe. It was reported that the present sensors shows the detection limit of 0.6 µM with the linear range of 1–100 µM. Although the AIE based sensors have several advantages over the routine optical probe that works on the quenching mechanism of the sensing still the complicated fabrication route as well cost the major shortcomings of the present sensing route [150].

In the recent years plasmonic NPs i.e. Au, Ag, Cu etc. have gained a lot of interest in various fields of science and technology specially sensing application due to their unique photo-physical properties i.e. ease of functionalization, high surface to volume ratio, excellent chemical as well physical stability, size dependent optical properties etc. [17,22,151,152]. All these properties makes these NPs a promising candidate for the sensing application of pesticides.

Liang et al., 2020; have done synthesis and characterization of Au NPs for the detection of OPs, where they have prepared Au NPs by the Bovine serum albumin templet method and tap water, seawater, sewage, and cucumber juice was taken as the real sample. Later Au NPs was added in yeast-AChE solution giving the result for the morphology of Au NPs with the diameter of about 7 nm (analyzed by TEM) and the fluorescence assay based on Au NPs and yeast-AChE exhibited ultrasensitivity for the ultra-trace of OPs with LOD 0.003 pM and the linear range was 0.1–100 pM, proposing the method to be excellent reliability [153]. Liu et al., developed highly sensititive probe i.e. rhodamine B-capped Au NPs for detecting OPs and carbamate pesticides [154]. The sensing mechanism rely on the fact that both the pesticides prevent the activity of aAChE and prevents the formation of thiocholine. Similarly, adenosine triphosphate- and rhodamine B-functionalized Au NPs have been utilized for the detection of OPs with the detection limit of 37.0 nM [155]. Tseng et al., fabricated rhodamine B decorated Au NPs for the efficient detection of thiodicarb [156]. The PL intensity of Au NPs is quenched by the rodamine B molecule via. FRET mechanism (electrostatic interaction) but when thiodicarb is added to system, PL intensity recovers due to adsorption competition between rhodamine B and thiodicarb. Thiodicarb have higher affinity towards Au NPs leads to the regeneration of PL signals. Similarly various reports are present in the literature for the efficient utilization of Au NPs for the detection of different pesticides [157]. Li et al., have done synthesis of 2-Mercaptobenzothiazole-Polyvinylpyrrolidone Cu NPs for the qualitative assessment of α-naphthaleneacetic. As for the study of NAA detection, the concentration of NAA was spiked into the apple juice. The detection limit (LOD) is ∼9.6 nM and the linear range was 0.5–50 µM, which reveals the PL strategy to be an excellent, convenient operation and promising tool for real-time monitoring of α-naphthaleneacetic [158]. Zhao et al. have fabricated aptamer (poly T) functionalized Cu NPs for the sensing application of omethoate with the detection limit of 0.22 nM [159]. The major issues with these NPs is the costlier precursors for the synthesis of these noble metal NPs. Further, Si NPs are a novel type of PL NPs that attracts various researcher’s attention due to their good optical, physical and chemical properties [160,161]. Si-NPs, mainly containing crystal silicon and its related oxides, and also can be taken as a zero-dimensional PL nanomaterial, providing the potential application in the field of batteries, optical devices, bioimaging, photovoltaics, and sensor, revealing time-saving, easy operation, cheaply available and applicable for detecting pesticides and other compounds [162,163].

For instance, Luo et al., prepared a Si NPs based RF ELISA for the quantification of EC in red wine sample and the result revealed that the Si NPs is colorless under visible light but exhibit strong blue emission under UV, the linear region was in the range of 0.39 − 10.5 µg/L, the LOD was found out to be 2.6 µg/L and recoveries of 86.0–102.70%, with a coefficient of variance 11.6% establish that the RF-ELISA could regulate EC concentration in red wine exhibiting excellent specificity, with accuracy, and good reproducibility [164]. Several other reports are present in literature that illustrates the implementation of Si NPs as PL sensor for different pesticides [165]. Not only metal NPs but various other forms of NPs have also been used in the sensing application of pesticides.

Liu et al., have preferred the AHNSA probe with MnO₂ nanosheets for the determination of MP. 4-amino-3-hydroxyl-1-naphthalenesul formic acid (AHNSA) taken as a PL nanoprobe due to its strong PL intensity at 460 nm ( Fig. 6).
Fig. 6.

For the fluorescence signaling, the MP was detected with MnO₂ NS attached with AChE and acetylthiocholine iodide (ATCh) and then added with AHNSA to the reaction system [35]. The existence of OPs will constrain the activity of AChE, thus avoiding the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine iodide but only to some degree. Due to the reduction of TCh concentration, the reduced NPs to Mn2+ is hindered, leading to PL quenching. In these systems, the fluorescence intensity of AHNSA is affected significantly. To examine the practicability of monitoring other pesticides 2,2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate was selected to analyze, the PL intensity quenched linearly by increasing PM concentration (0.4–40 ng/mL) with LOD of 0.18 ng/mL was achieved and indicating the PL assay used for monitoring the OPs with the aid of AHNSA and MnO₂ NPs has superior sensing properties as compare to other published reports. For the real sample detection, lake water samples were added to the different concentrations of MP achieving the rate of recovery in real water samples and the results demonstrates the excellent applicability of proposed methods in practical samples.

When we consider the optical application of NPs, especially PL application of NPs, quantum dots are proven to be the ideal candidates due to high PL intensity, size dependent emission etc. Quantum Dots (QDs), due to their unique structure and electronic configuration, have excellent optical and electronic properties [166]. Due to quantum confinement, QDs exhibit excellent PL fluorescent that leads to various application particularly PL fluorescent probes have attracted in many fields for their applications as optical devices, biosensors and biological imaging [167]. Till now, various metal based as well as metal free QDs have been synthesized for the sensing application of various pesticides some of them are discussed below [168,169]. In this context, F.-Kashani et al., have successfully developed MoS2 QDs for the efficient detection of MP in rice [170]. The whole sensing mechanism depends on the hydrolysis of the MP in basic mechanism that leads to the formation of para nitrophenol that can be utilized for the detection of the MP. Resonance energy transfer from MoS2 QDs to paranitrophenol leads to the change in PL signal and the sensing parameters comes out to be 0.085 µg/mL. Similarly, Yi et al., functionalized MoS2 QDs with β-Cyclodextrin as a nansensor for the detection of MP [171]. The mechanism of functionalization of CDs to MoS2 QDs have been schematically explained in the Fig. 7.

β-CD are water-soluble cyclic oligosaccharides comprise of 6–8 units of glucopyranose and the probe generally can be considered as water-soluble fluorophore [53,172]. The examination of MP was achieved through the incorporation of β-CD-MoS₂ QDs with para-nitrophenol produced via hydrolytic action of MP and fluorescence intensity was measured with consistent photo-induced electron transfer or resonance energy transfer or process achieving the PL fluorescent lifetime of β-CD-MoS₂ QDs to be significantly rapid after the addition of para-nitrophenol, validating the PET through quenching mechanism. The linear response has been shown form 0.01–18 ppm between the PL fluorescent intensity and concentration of MP with the detection limit of 3.3 ppb. The most important part of the present system is the enzyme less sensing that works on host-guest recognition. In addition, different real sample have been spiked including apple and cucumber spiked with different concentrations, exhibiting good recoveries (93–105.6%) with a minimum RSD (4.42%) which indicates the planned PL fluorescent nanosensors has significant potential applicability, feasibility, and excellent selectivity.

Several other various pesticides have been successful monitored by using CdTe QDs, Si QDs, CdS QDs [173–176]. To further improve the PL intensity various researcher doped with metal ions as well fabricate the heterostructures with other QDs. In this context, several studies were reported on the doping as well heterostructures of different QDs i.e. Mn+2 doped ZnSe QDs, Mn+2 doped ZnS QDs, CdSe/ZnS QDs, CdSe/ZnSe QDs, CdTe/CdS QDs were also been explored for the sensing of various pesticides [111,177–180].

The major issue with these QDs is the biocompatibility. With time, these is leaching issue of the metal ions in the solution that enhances the toxicity of metal based QDs. to overcome this issue recently carbonaceous QDs and there hybrid structures are widely explored in the optical application specially sensing due to unmatched properties such as high water stability, chemical and physical stability, high quantum yield, ease of functionalization, resistance towards photo bleaching ease of synthesis etc. [181–183].

Tafreshi et, al., have synthesized C dots from plant extract and utilize it in the sensing of amicarbazone, glyphosate, and diazinon. The detection limit comes out to be 2, 0.5, 0.25 ng/mL for glyphosate, amicarbazone and diazinon pesticides respectively. The present sensors works quite well in real samples [184]. Carbaryl pesticide have been detection with the CDs with excellent selectivity of 0.0268 nM and the sensor is successful implement on the real sample of apple [185]. Similarly, C Dots were also implemented for the detection of glyphosate detection [124]. Liang et al. developed a smartphone-based visualization device for the visible monitoring of malathion. This system consists of a lightbox along with smartphone and lightbox with black paper to obtain the colorimetric images [186]. Fig. 8 shows the absorption and emission sensing of malathion using CQDs. A good linear range from 1 nM to 10 mM with the LOD of 0.13 nM (fluorescence) and 0.59 nM (colorimetric) revealing the the colorimetric sensing have better sensing results as compare to PL.

They have also developed the CQDs and Au NPs based nano-sensor by synthesis of CQDs and GNPs and preparing typical arrays combined with a phone and calibration curves. Au NPs gained interest due to their low cost, availability and show great potential in optical, electrical, chemical, senor, bioremediation, and biochemical fields [187]. For the detection of malathion in a real sample, cabbage was contaminated by malathion in order to evaluate the practical application of CQDs and Au NPs. In addition, the aptamer combined with malathion and Au NPs gets aggregated resulting in the visible change in color (red to blue) as well as PL intensity. Standard arrays of fluorescence and colorimetric combined with smartphones were found to be portable and convenient, consisting of images of the standard arrays with different concentrations of malathion. Thus, representative the visual recognition of malathion to be advantageous to the on spot and rapid detection of malathion. The established standard arrays and the calibration curve ranges from 1 mM to 10 mM and the LOD was estimated to be 0.13 nM. The recoveries of calibration curves detection were 89.9%− 103.4% (fluorescence) and 88.7%− 107.6% (colorimetric) specifying the CQDs-GNPs based nano-sensor to be selective and sensitive properties for the detection of malathion. This PL probe can be synthesized by various materials in green routes such as leaves and vegetables, they also can provide a carbon source for detecting pesticides, small biomolecules, antibiotics, and metal ions [188–190]. The precursor compound materials do not contain any heavy metal elements, which would lessen the impact on the environment [191]. In the field of electronics, catalysts, and biology, CDs owned a high potential, replacing metal based QDs and conventional dyes. The synthesis of CDs through chemical oxidation, pyrolytic carbonization and simple hydrothermal treatment carried adequate active functional groups that facilitates the facile bioconjugation onto the surface of CDs [161]. Several CDs based composites as well as heteroatom doped CDs have been used to improve the optical electrochemical and electrical, and also the fluorescence efficiency of CDs shows noteworthy enhancement in their quantum yield (QY) over 80% [187]. Thus, due to the advanced synthesis, diverse functionalization, and innovative application of CDs are in research hotspot [158,192–194].

Li et al., have prepared three co-incorporated CDs that contains nitrogen, sulfur and flavonoid moiety co-incorporated CD (NS-CD-Fla), nitrogen and flavonoid moiety co-incorporated CD (N-CD-Fla), and nitrogen, sulfur, boron, and flavonoid moiety co-incorporated CD (NSB-CD-Fla) using Ginkgo biloba leave as the carbon source for sensitive PL sensing of pesticides by taking rice as the real sample. Some pesticides and their analog i.e. dinosenb dithianon and fenitrothion were explored for three CDs-based PL investigations [30]. Then the PL spectra of suspension were examined by the PL, where three CDs displayed clear UV–vis absorption peak and exhibited yellow-green luminescence, excitation at 400 nm (N-CD-Fla), blue luminescence, excitation at 340 nm (NS-CD-Fla), and blue-green emission, excitation at 390 nm (NSB-CD-Fla) respectively. NS-CD-Fla displayed the strongest blue light emission and gave the maximum quantum yield of 46.6%, higher 33.8% of blue-green NSB-CD-Fla higher than 26.2% of yellow-green N-CD-Fla and. The LOD of Fen, Dit, and Din were estimated to be 0.36, 0.28, and 0.66 nM at the signal-to-noise ratio of 3, the recovery was in the ranges of 91–100.5%, 92.9–101.6%, and 91.3–102.3%, and the quenching efficiency displayed desirable linear relationship versus the concentration in the range of 1–2000 nM, 1–100 nM and 1–3000 nM, respectively which suggest that the sensing method gives desirable detection limit, precision, and accuracy [30]. The result revealed reasonable recovery, sensitivity, and selectivity, excellent LOD, and the detection method has potential applicability for pesticide sensing from samples. Thus, the multiple doping CDs have promising application potential for pesticides detection. Nair et al., have performed one experiment where they have synthesized Sulphur- GQDs (S-GQDs) from graphene oxide (GO) with KMnO₄ and H₂SO₄ and the result exhibited a color change from blue to green as compare to the undoped GQDs [76]. For the detection of pesticides, a prepared aqueous solution of S-GQDs was used and fabricated with poly (vinyl) alcohol (PVA), two carbamate pesticides for real sample, apple was selected. Later the result represents digital photos of blue-colored GQDs and green-colored S-GQDs irradiated upon a 365 nm UV lamp. The LOD was found out to be 0.45 ppb and 1.6 ppb for CF and Tr respectively and the linear range was from 0 to 10ppb for Cr. The result exhibited, PVA/S-GQDs has confirmed as an exceptional PL sensor for the ultra-high sensitive assessment of pesticides and can be applied at trace-level for environment monitoring and food safety. The major issue with NPs particularly with QDs is the selectivity, there must be some ligand required on the surface of QDs for enhancing the selectivity towards the specific entity. In addition, biocompatibility issues are there metal based QDs due metal ion leaching.

Polymers incorporated molecularly imprinting i.e. molecular Imprinting polymers (MIPs), also known as synthetic antibodies, are polymeric synthetic materials with specific cavity associated with the given templates and observation areas, which directly bind to molecules. MIPs are usually compiled by copolymerization of active and compact monomers where there is a target/template, and polymerization. When template is extracted, these specially designed artifacts are capable of direct attention and corresponding printing holes with shape, size, and functional group equal to the target molecule built into MIPs, allowing the template to re-enter MIPs. Recently, MIPs have been gradually developed as well as fine-tuning devices with specific chemical and geometrical compounds in a targeted molecule. MIPs have received attention in the field of biosensors, drug delivery, and catalysis. It has made great strides in the use of nanomaterials as signal transducer elements due to its large surface-to-volume dimensions, unique physicochemical features, and easy modification. MIPs are integrated with molecular imprinting techniques and this technique is considered to be effective in improving fluorescence-based MIPs acquisition. MIPs also have high mechanical and chemical stability and are inexpensive. However, MIPs have many limitations, including incomplete template removal, low binding volume, low affinity, and unusual material conditions. To overcome these limitations, the surface molecular imprinted technique was introduced and considered as an effective method, which constructed and integrated certain binding sites on or near the material to improve bulk transfer and reduce static penetration. Thus, MIPs have attracted a great deal of interest in the acquisition of pesticides because of their excellent properties [85,86,195–200]. H. Li et al., group has prepared a SiO₂/ZnO/MIPs fluorescence sensor for detection of pyrethroids taking river water as a real sample. The LOD was 0.13 µmol/L, the linear range was determined to be 1–80 µmol/L and the recovery was from 93.30% to 106.13% which proves that the fluorescence sensor has high QD sensitivity and high MIP selection and can be used in other fields including agriculture, forestry, and urban pollution research [85,200]. The graphical presentation of the sensing system in illustrated in Fig. 9.

QDs have been widely used as the optical probe or signal transducers for the decorations of MIPs as receptor towards specific entity. Several reports present in literature on QDs functionalized MIPs for the detection of several pesticide [176,201–211]. In this context, Ren and Chen choose ZnS QDs doped with Mn and decorated it with MIPs to develop the sensor for cyphenothrin pesticide with the limit of detection of 9.0 nM in aqueous system [204]. The same optical probe has been utilized with different functionality to generate the sensing of nicosulfuron pesticides with the detection limit of 1.1 nM [205] and chlorpyrifos with the detection limit of 17 nM [212]. Silica embedded QDs have also been used as optical probe for the functionalization of MIPs. In particular, CdSe QDs have been embedded on the silica spheres and modified with MIP and has been used for the detection of 1-cyhalothrin in aqueous system with the detection limit of 80.03 nM [206]. Similarly, CdTe doped silica spheres functionalized with MIPs were utilized for the efficient detection of delta-methrin present in vegetables as fruits [207]. Although MIPs have been successfully explored for the sensing application still there are some shortcoming in there implementation i.e. extraction of template form the polymer, time consuming, costlier as compare to other, and most importantly the fabrication of the MIPs requires a lot of solvent for the extraction of templates [169]. These shortcoming must needed for the commercialization of the present sensing technique.

Ratiometric Fluorescence Nanoprobe (RF) this method basically focused change in the relative ratio of two different PL emission when excited at the single wavelength. The main advantage of this method is it owns a self-calibration parameters that eliminates the PL signal generated by the environment (false PL signals), and thus displays better accuracy and sensitivity [164]. In addition, it overcomes some critical issues related to the background PL emission, concentration of fluorophore, as well as environmental issues that disturb the PL intensity. RF strategy provides better sensing platform by providing visible changes in color in presence and absence of the target molecule as shown in Fig. 10 [77,213].

These results leads to the development of nano sensors for visual tracking particularly on-site for pesticides that is of great importance [214]. However, it still lacks in intensity amplification along with potential selectivity and interference concerns in the broad application of ratiometric sensing [215,216]. Qu et al., 2020; group have prepared dual lanthanide-probe based on coordination polymer network (CPNs), where both of guanylate monophosphate and 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid were synthesized along with Eu for ratiometric sensing of GLY in food samples (rice, millet, milk and soybean). The fluorescence emission of GMP/Eu/DPA exhibited red fluorescence under the excitation of 270 nm [190,217] The ratiometric detection of alkaline phosphate (ALP) has resulted in LOD 0.08 U/L and the linear range of ALP is from 0.1 to 300 U/L. Thus, the result reveals that the proposed method is a perfect diagnostic methodology in the assessment of GLY in various foods stuff.

Fluorescent chemosensors is one of the research hotspots in analytical chemistry, chemical biology, and biomedical engineering and it has received great attention due to high selectivity, sensitivity, specificity, extremely economical, simple operation, exhibit naked-eye recognition, non-destructive and real-time analysis [20,218–221]. FC has become more prominent, especially for the detection of heavy metals, environmental pollutants, and pesticides [222–227]. Wu et al., 2020; group have prepared a dual-modal colorimetric and PL chemosensor for detection of fluoride anions based on 1,8-naphthalimide,2-(2,2-dichloroacetamide)-N-n-butyl-naphthalimide in CH₃CN. The LOD for F⁻ determination resulted to be 1.41 µM and have a good linear relationship over the range of 15–50 µM of F⁻. And the LOD for oxyfluorfen was resulted to be 0.38 µM, which was low enough. The result implied that the fluorescence was reliable and could be used to determine the pesticides in a real application [227].

ECL Sensor or electro generated chemiluminescence is a combination of electrochemistry and chemiluminescence [228,229]. Due to its versatility, has gained popularity in the field of electrochemical and optical nanosensor, catalysis, bioimaging, and drug delivery. ECL involves the production of organic matter in the electrode area where the ECL luminophore receives high-energy electron-transfer reactions to form electronically pleasing regions that emit bright light [230]. As an analytical technique, it converts electrical energy into radioactive energy through the production of active mediums from stable precursors on the electrode [231]. ECL exhibits advantages such as highly sensitive, highly selective method, simplified set-up, fast and convenient measurement control, minimal electrode fouling, wide linear range, low-cost tool, advanced labeling immunoassay technique, and zero background, due to which ECL has become a powerful detection tool for detecting pesticides, pharmaceutical analysis, and different analytes but still the ECL methodology certain drawback such as experimental set up, cost efficacy [229,232–237].

G. Yang et al., The team worked on a ratiometric ECL biosensor based on Ir nanorods and CdS quantum dots to detect OP pesticides wirh the detection limit is estimated at 1.67 pM. They took pakchoi, cabbage, and lettuce as actual samples to test the possible biosensor measurement performance to determine when ethyl paraoxon (EP) recovery values were 91.9∼98.7% in pakchoi sample, 101∼108% in cabbage sample and 93.3∼101% in lettuce sample, thus demonstrating excellent EP detection and analysis of active samples [229]. Liang et al., have developed novel ECL biosensor based on graphene, CdTe QDs, and acetylcholinesterase for the sensing of OPs and the detection limit was reported to be 0.06 ng/mL [238]. Similarly, upconverting nanoparticle functionalized with zeolite imidazolate framework and functionalized with MIPs to develop the efficient ECL sensor for successfully achieve the quantitative detection of imidacloprid and the limit of detection reported to be 0.01 ng/mL [239].

Cucurbit[n]urils (abbreviated as Q[n]s), a class of synthetic macrocyclic receptor or host molecule, constitute n glycoluril units and 2 n methylene groups [240]. The shape of Q[n]s seems to be more like a pumpkin, having a hydrophilic exterior and hydrophobic interior that derived encapsulation of the fluorophores and interact with various molecules such as specific amino acid, peptides, and proteins, through host-guest interaction [241,242]. Q[n]s have neutral hydrophobic cavities and negatively charged carbonyl-laced portals, where the cavity is non-polar and have a rigid structure, can bind to specific guest molecules through hydrophobic interaction and as the carbonyl portals are electron-rich, they are suitable for binding to the positively charged molecules via ion-dipole interaction [243–245]. The emission characteristics of guest molecules and also exhibits excellent water solubility, which is found to be applicable in an aqueous system [13,246,247].

Y. Cao et al., group have prepared PL probe based on cucurbit[6]uril modified CdTe QDs (CB[6]-QDs) for selective analysis of p-nitroaniline in environmental samples. It showed fluorescence response to PNA giving bright green fluorescence. Then the PNA was determined in a different environmental sample such as lake water and lake soil where LOD was estimated to be 60 µmol/L, linear range 0.25–3.0 mol/L, and recovery rate were in the range of 88–109%. Thus, this fluorescence sensor proves to be a simple, selective, sensitive method, and applicable for the detection of environmental samples [240]. Similarly, a novel PL probe based on cucurbit[10]uril and aminopropyl-1-pyrenebutanamide have been developed for the sensing of dodine in 20 different pesticides [248]. Wu et al., have developed a PL sensor using cucurbit[8]uril and (E)− 1-(2-hydroxyethyl)− 4-(4-hydroxystyryl)pyridin-1-ium bromide for monitoring tricyclazole pesticide with the detection limit was found to be 37 nM [249]. the major shortcoming of cucurbit[8]uril as optical sensor is to tune the binding preference by synthetic means that makes the synthesis route more complicated.

MOFs are the self-assembled coordination polymers with porous nature in which the organic ligands that coordinates with metal ions [250,251]. These MOFs shows diverse application due to its ease in functionalization. In addition to their diverse applications, MOFs, due to the aromatic subunits, are also explored as an optical probe for the sensing application [251].

In this context, Wang et. al., have developed the luminescent Eu ion based MOFs for the detection of 2,4-dichloro-4-nitroaniline with the limit of detection of 5.28 mg/L with the repeatability of 5 cycles ( Fig. 11) [252].

The same analyte has been monitored with N-bis (isophthalic acid) oxalamide ligand MOFs with the better detection limit i.e. 3.85 mg/L [253]. Fan et al. have developed the Zn based MOFs for the sensing application of 2,4-dichloro-4-nitroaniline with the detection limit of 116 ppb [254].

Cui et al., have synthesized Tb based MOFs for the detection of thiamethoxam, and imidacloprid with the detection limit of 7.3 µM and 13 µM respectively [255]. Co based MOFs have been explored for the determination of residues of pesticides such as nitrophenyl and p nitrophenyl phosphate groups in the various juices [256]. Efforts have also been made in the encapsulation of Au NPs in MOFs that works on AIE mechanism and explored for the detection of OPs. Similarly Mn doped ZnS QDs encapsulated with MOFs and functionalized with MIPs for the detection of Chlorpyrifos with the detection limit of 0.89 µmol/L [257]. GLY has been successfully detected by the aid of Cd based MOFs in dimethyl formamide medium with the detection limit of 0.025 µmol/L [258]. Furthermore, MP sensors have been developed by the Zr based MOFs that provides sensing in aqueous medium with the detection limit of 0.115 µg/L [259]. Zn based MOFs have been developed for the sensing application of parathion in the irrigation water that works quite efficiently for 15 days [260]. Although, MOFs are widely explored in the optical sensing still majority of MOFs have been utilized in the non-aqueous medium that limits their utility from environmental point of view [261].
Various optical sensors for the detection of number of pesticides have been compiled in Table 1.

In recent years, FPADs have exhibited promising results in terms of public health, food safety inspection, and monitoring environmental contaminants due to biocompatibility, effectiveness, easy fabrication, on-site detection, economical, flexible, and portable platform, and disposability [87,263–266]. Due to the fact that paper is porous and hydrophilic in nature, it works as an excellent platform for reagent storage and facilitates fluid transport. It is considered to be a distance-based device because it uses length or distance in quantitative measurements and prints a simple scale on the same device for quantifying concentration. In comparison to electrochemical and colorimetric PADs. However, the biggest challenge for developing FPADs is to prepare highly efficient and stable solid-state PL materials [194,267–270].

Chen et al., group have prepared an FPAD based on AIE NPs and 2D MnO₂ nanoflakes for recognition of OPs. Where AIE NPs gave a bright blue-green fluorescence [267]. AIE NPs and 2D MnO₂ nanoflakes were employed to sensitively detect OPs in solution with a LOD of 0.027 ng/mL, and were employed to fabricate PMn- FPAD for instrument-free and visual detection of OPs with a LOD of 0.73 ng/mL and linear range found to be 0–300 ng/mL. Thus, the result exhibited that the proposed sensor is applicable in the detection of OPs in the practical sample and suggests excellent performance and strong practicability. In another study Chen et al., have developed a novel paper based sensor with the aid of CdTe QDs along with nano zinc 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra(4-pyridyl)− 21–23 H-porphine for the efficient, facile, cost effective visual sensing of carbaryl, carbofuran, and metolcarb detection. The present sensor works quite well with different food stuff [7]. Similarly, Kavruk et al., have developed a paper biased sensors using AChE and 2-nitro-5-thiobenzoic acid for the detection of malathion with the detection limit of 2.5 ppm [271]. The major drawback of the present sensing system is its temperature for storage for further use i.e. 40 C. Cu@Ag core shell NPs was also explored for the development of paper based optical sensor for phenthoate pesticide [272]. Although this sensing methodology carries several advantages like portability, cost effectiveness, on spot detection, still the leaching the probe form surface, interference of the surrounding as well as paper materials, monitoring of the signal is major drawback of the these type of sensors.

The present review focused on the recent advancements in the fields of luminescent sensors for the quantitative and qualitative assessment of pesticides that offers superior selectivity as well as sensitivity. The review is classified the various categories of PL sensors for pesticides such biochemical based sensors, NPs based sensors, MIPs based sensors, MOFs based sensors, and finally the paper based sensors. Pristine NPs provides the better platform as compare to the conventional sensors but the major issue for using the pristine NPs/QDs is the weak selectivity over diverse pesticides. To overcome the issue of the selectivity NPs/QDs have been functionalized with various biochemical entities that provide excellent selectively. In case of optical biosensors (Aptamers, antibodies, AIE), to introduce the luminescence different types of optical NPs have been introduced in the biological moieties. Although excellent sensitivity, along with superior selectivity have been reported in the these sensors still the major issues with these type of sensors are the activity of enzyme or biochemical are prone to the surrounding atmosphere (pH, temperature, pre and post conditions for biochemicals etc.), cost of biochemicals and tedious synthetic and handling route. MIPs on the other hand provides a better alternate towards improving the specificity as well selectivity due the absence of any biochemicals in its structure but It also requires the signal transducers for the optical signals. Therefore, both the biochemicals as well as MIPs requires the optical entity it its core. The major shortcoming in MIPs based sensors is the in situ polymerization reaction in presence of target moiety that is not only a tedious process but also the polymer fabricated might be irregular that could trap the imprinted impression leading to poor adsorption for target analyte. In addition, the pre and post condition of cavity generation in the polymer is quite complicated. Finally the MOFs as optical sensors have been reviewed. The PL properties in MOFs is form the aromatic subunit with the structure or some time the optical properties is introduced by the external sources particularly NPs. But to achieve the selectively, MIPs are introduced on the surface making the system more complicated. In addition, majority of the MOFs requires non-aqueous system that limit their use from environmental point of view. Overall there is still more improvement required in term of biocompatibility, cost effectiveness, easy to handle, easy to commercialize, on site workability etc. for the luminescent sensing of pesticides.

Aswini Gyanjyoti: Data curation, Writing – original draft preparation. Praveen Guleria: Conceptualization, Methodology. Abhishek Awasthi: Visualization. Kulvinder Singh: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision. Vineet Kumar: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Resources.
